food_subgroup: Steamed shellfish — Venus clams (la la; Anomalodiscus squamosus / Paphia undulata) steamed with garlic, ginger, and Chinese rice wine

ontology.foodon_gap_note: Closest parent: clam as food product (FOODON_03309810). No FoodOn URI exists for steamed clam dish with garlic and rice wine specifically.
ontology.foodex2_description: Clams and cockles (Veneridae / Cardiidae) — steamed shellfish preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJL covers clams and cockles as a shellfish category. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for the Singapore zi char steamed preparation with garlic and rice wine. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for steamed clam dish. SNOMED CT covers clinical entities; this food preparation does not have a confirmed clinical food code.

serving.description: 1 portion — approximately 300g whole with shells (~120g edible clam meat and sauce)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard zi char or hawker serving of steamed la la is approximately 300g whole with shells, yielding roughly 120g of edible clam meat and sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Clams, mixed species, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174202); cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient database for steamed shellfish preparation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (SR Legacy) / Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy clam (mixed species, raw) used as primary nutrient anchor. Values adjusted for steamed preparation (minimal nutrient loss for shellfish steaming). Sodium reflects addition of light soy sauce and natural clam sodium as documented in HPB Singapore foodo.sg steamed shellfish entries. Vitamin B12 value (98 mcg/100g) is consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for clams, which report exceptionally high B12 concentrations (84–98 mcg/100g depending on species). Iron (13 mg/100g) is consistent with USDA SR Legacy clam data.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index principles for very-low-carbohydrate shellfish foods — analogous estimate; steamed clams contain 1.5g carbohydrate per 100g (primarily glycogen) yielding negligible glycemic impact
gi_source.source_match_notes: Steamed clams contain only 1.5g carbohydrate per 100g (primarily glycogen). No direct GI test exists for steamed clams in the University of Sydney GI Database. A nominal GI of 5 is assigned as an analogous estimate consistent with very-low-carbohydrate shellfish and protein-dominant foods; this reflects negligible glycemic impact. Glycemic load per 120g serving = (1.5 × 1.2) × (5/100) = 0.09, rounded to 0.1. The University of Sydney GI Database is the authoritative GI reference; no specific clam entry exists, hence analogous_estimate classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (170 mg/100g), high potassium (310 mg/100g) — favourable Na:K ratio for blood pressure management
hypertension.guidance: At 170 mg sodium per 100g (204 mg per 120g edible serving), steamed la la is among the lowest-sodium seafood dishes available at Singapore hawker and zi char stalls; the high potassium content (310 mg/100g) further supports blood pressure control, making this dish suitable for hypertensive patients when soy sauce is used minimally.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (1.5g/100g), very high protein (15g/100g), low calorie density (80 kcal/100g) — minimal glycaemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Steamed clams have negligible carbohydrate content and a glycaemic load of essentially zero per serving, making them an excellent high-protein, low-calorie choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes who require blood glucose control without sacrificing protein intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.3g/100g), low total fat (1.7g/100g), zero trans fat, high omega-associated shellfish profile; cholesterol moderate at 50 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steamed clams are very low in saturated fat and total fat, contain no trans fat, and provide cardioprotective minerals (potassium, magnesium, selenium); the moderate dietary cholesterol (50 mg/100g) is not a primary concern for most cardiovascular patients under current dietary guidelines, and the high protein-to-calorie ratio supports lean body mass maintenance relevant to CVD risk reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Exceptionally high iron (13 mg/100g) and vitamin B12 (98 mcg/100g) — critical for RA patients with anaemia and those on methotrexate
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Steamed clams provide 13 mg iron per 100g (exceeding the daily RDA in a single 120g serving) and 98 mcg vitamin B12 per 100g (approximately 4,900% of daily RDA), making them clinically valuable for rheumatoid arthritis patients with anaemia of chronic disease or those on methotrexate therapy, which depletes folate-B12 metabolism.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Clams with Garlic and Chinese Rice Wine (蒜蓉蒸啦啦 / Suàn Róng Zhēng Lā Lā) is a classic Singapore zi char and Teochew-Cantonese seafood dish featuring Venus clams (la la; Anomalodiscus squamosus or Paphia undulata) steamed live in their shells with aromatic garlic, fresh ginger, and Chinese rice wine (Shaoxing or rice wine). The dish is finished with a light touch of soy sauce and sometimes sesame oil. The clam flesh is tender, slightly chewy, and naturally sweet with a clean oceanic flavour enhanced by the fragrant garlic-wine steam. A standard hawker or zi char portion is approximately 300g whole with shells, yielding roughly 120g of edible clam meat and sauce. Nutritionally, steamed la la is one of the most remarkable dishes available in Singapore: per 120g edible serving it provides approximately 18g protein, 15.6mg iron (meeting or exceeding the daily iron RDA in a single dish), and 118 mcg vitamin B12 — approximately 4,900% of the daily recommended intake. Clams contain the highest dietary concentration of vitamin B12 of any commonly available food, rivalling or exceeding beef liver. They are also rich in selenium (25 mcg/100g), zinc (2 mg/100g), and phosphorus (200 mg/100g). Calorie density is very low at 80 kcal/100g (~96 kcal per serving), total fat is minimal (1.7g/100g), and sodium is moderate at 170 mg/100g (204 mg per serving). The dish is not halal (shellfish) and not suitable for kosher diets. It is dairy-free. Clinically, steamed clams are ideal for Type 2 Diabetes (negligible glycaemic load), hypertension (low sodium, high potassium), cardiovascular disease (very low saturated fat, zero trans fat), and rheumatoid arthritis (exceptional iron and B12 for anaemia and methotrexate support). Available at zi char restaurants, hawker seafood stalls, and in home Teochew-Cantonese cooking across Singapore.
common_user_queries: steamed clams garlic rice wine calories

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced from USDA SR Legacy (clams, mixed species) — an approved primary source — and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo steamed shellfish entries. Vitamin B12 (98 mcg/100g) and iron (13 mg/100g) are consistent with published USDA SR Legacy clam data. Sodium reflects steamed preparation with minimal soy sauce addition. All values are internally consistent and plausible for the described preparation.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for steamed Venus clam preparation not separately reported in USDA SR Legacy or HPB Singapore database as of 2026-04-13; USDA SR Legacy reports total fat only for mixed-species clams in this preparation context. water_g set to -1: moisture content for steamed clam preparation not reported in the primary source consulted. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy clam data; shellfish contain no trans fats by natural composition. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — naturally zero; clam flesh contains no dietary fibre. Glycemic index assigned as nominal value of 5 (analogous estimate) reflecting negligible carbohydrate content (1.5g/100g, primarily glycogen); no direct GI test for steamed clams exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; glycemic load per 120g serving is 0.1. Halal status: NOT halal — shellfish (molluscs) are not permitted under standard halal dietary rules. Not suitable for kosher diets. The extraordinary vitamin B12 content (98 mcg/100g) is well-documented for clams in USDA SR Legacy and is not an error; clams are the single highest dietary B12 source among commonly available foods.
